elements.sent-2411-001: In a variety of domains, from robotics to finance, Quality-Diversity algorithms have been used to generate collections of both diverse and high-performing solutions.
elements.sent-2411-002: Multi-Objective Quality-Diversity algorithms have emerged as a promising approach for applying these methods to complex, multi-objective problems.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, existing methods are limited by their search capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-004: For example, Multi-Objective Map-Elites depends on random genetic variations which struggle in high-dimensional search spaces.
elements.sent-2411-005: Despite efforts to enhance search efficiency with gradient-based mutation operators, existing approaches consider updating solutions to improve on each objective separately rather than achieving desired trade-offs.
elements.sent-2411-006: In this work, we address this limitation by introducing Multi-Objective Map-Elites with Preference-Conditioned Policy-Gradient and Crowding Mechanisms: a new \moqdlong algorithm that uses preference-conditioned policy-gradient mutations to efficiently discover promising regions of the objective space and crowding mechanisms to promote a uniform distribution of solutions on the Pareto front.
elements.sent-2411-007: We evaluate our approach on six robotics locomotion tasks and show that our method outperforms or matches all state-of-the-art \moqdlong methods in all six, including two newly proposed tri-objective tasks.
elements.sent-2411-008: Importantly, our method also achieves a smoother set of trade-offs, as measured by newly-proposed sparsity-based metrics.
elements.sent-2411-009: This performance comes at a lower computational storage cost compared to previous methods.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width =\hsize]{Abstract-intro-conclusion/figures/teaser.jpg} \caption{Left. Multi-Objective MAP-Elites repertoire. The feature space $\mathcal{C} \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ is tessellated into cells $\mathcal{C}_i$. A Pareto Front is stored in each cell. The aim of \moqd algorithms is to fill each cell with solutions that are Pareto-optimal. Right. Overview of preference-conditioned policy gradient in the \pcc algorithm. By conditioning policy-gradients on updates solutions can be improved toward achieving different trade-offs of objectives (illustrated by blue arrows). By contrast, in \pgx, solutions are only updated to improve performance on each objective separately (illustrated by light blue arrows).} \label{fig:teaser}
elements.sent-2411-010: Over recent years, Deep Reinforcement Learning (RL) has enabled breakthroughs in mastering games~\cite{alphago, dqn as well as continuous control domains for locomotion~\cite{smith2022walk, cheng2023extreme and manipulation~\cite{openai2019solving .}}}
elements.sent-2411-011: These milestones have demonstrated the extraordinary potential of RL algorithms to solve specific problems.
elements.sent-2411-012: However, most approaches return only one highly-specialised solution to a single problem.
elements.sent-2411-013: In contrast, there is a growing shift in focus towards not just uncovering one single solution that achieves high rewards, but instead many solutions that exhibit different ways of doing so \cite{alphazero_db .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Within this context, Quality-Diversity (\qd) algorithms \cite{qdunifying have emerged as one promising approach for tackling this challenge.}
elements.sent-2411-015: In \qd, the primary goal is to produce a variety of high-quality solutions, rather than to focus exclusively on finding the single best one.
elements.sent-2411-016: One motivation for \qd algorithms is that, finding many solutions can provide availability of alternative, back-up solutions in the event that the highest-performing solution is no longer suitable.
elements.sent-2411-017: For example, in robotics, generating large collections of solutions has been shown to be helpful for addressing large simulation to reality gaps \cite{nature and adapting to unforeseen damages \cite{nature, hbr .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Alternatively, having multiple solutions can simply be used in order to promote innovation in the downstream task.
elements.sent-2411-019: In this context, \qd has been used for creating diverse video game levels \cite{videogames,mario_videogames and generating building designs \cite{mcx .}}
elements.sent-2411-020: Despite the growing traction of \qd, most research in this field has focused on single-objective applications.
elements.sent-2411-021: However, multi-objective (\mo) problems pervade many real-world domains, including engineering \cite{mo_satellites, mo_windturbines , finance \cite{mo_finance , and drug design \cite{mo_molecular and many state-of-the-art \mo algorithms originate from Evolutionary Algorithm community \cite{spea, spea2, nsga2, moea/d .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: In \moqd, the overarching goal is to identify a broad collection of solutions that exhibit diverse features and achieve distinct performances across multiple objectives.
elements.sent-2411-023: More specifically, given a feature space that is tessellated into cells, the aim is to find a collection of solutions within each cell which offer different trade-offs on each of the objectives (see \Cref{fig:teaser ).}
elements.sent-2411-024: As an example, consider the task of designing building sites.
elements.sent-2411-025: Within this context, it may be interesting to find different designs that vary in the number of buildings on the site.
elements.sent-2411-026: Then for each possible number of buildings, further options can be generated which present different trade-offs of ventilation and noise levels \cite{mcx .}
elements.sent-2411-027: This approach equips end-users with a spectrum of viable options, thereby broadening their perspective on the array of feasible design possibilities.
elements.sent-2411-028: The \mome algorithm demonstrated promising results in finding large collections of diverse solutions that balance multiple objectives.
elements.sent-2411-029: However, \mome predominantly depends on random genetic variations that can cause slow convergence in large search spaces \cite{pga,pga_empirical_analysis,qdpg .}
elements.sent-2411-030: This renders it less suitable for evolving neural networks with a large number of parameters.
elements.sent-2411-031: Since the inception of the \mome framework, several related works exploring the domain of \moqd have emerged \cite{mome-pgx,mcx,move .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Among them, \pgx \cite{mome-pgx builds upon the \mome framework and was shown to achieve state-of-the-art performance on high-dimensional continuous control robotics tasks that can be framed as Markov Decision Processes.}
elements.sent-2411-033: It uses crowding addition and selection mechanisms to encourage an even distribution of solutions on the Pareto front and employs policy-gradient mutations for each objective function in order to drive the exploration process toward promising regions of the solution space.
elements.sent-2411-034: However, the \pgx approach is not without its own set of challenges.
elements.sent-2411-035: Firstly, it employs separate actor-critic networks for each objective function, which can be resource-intensive and may not scale with an increasing number of objectives.
elements.sent-2411-036: Furthermore, although using policy gradient-based updates helps with exploration in high-dimensional search spaces, the approach in \pgx only considers improving solutions on each objective separately.
elements.sent-2411-037: However, in the context of multi-objective problems, the goal is often not just to maximise each objective independently but rather to find solutions which offer different trade-offs among them.
elements.sent-2411-038: In this way, if end users have different preferences regarding the relative importance of each objective, they have a range of solutions to choose from.
elements.sent-2411-039: In this paper, we address the limitations of \pgx by introducing a new \moqd algorithm: Multi-Objective Map-Elites with Preference-Conditioned Policy-Gradient and Crowding Mechanisms (\pcc).
elements.sent-2411-040: Rather than using a separate actor-critic framework for each objective, \pcc uses a single preference-conditioned actor and a single preference-conditioned critic.
elements.sent-2411-041: Similar to \pgx, the actor-critic framework in \pcc can be used to provide policy-gradient mutations which offer efficient search space exploration for high-dimensional neural-network policies.
elements.sent-2411-042: However, as illustrated in \Cref{fig:teaser , by conditioning the actor and critic networks on a preference, policy-gradient updates can be used to improve solutions toward achieving a given weighting over the objectives, rather than improve solutions on each objective disjointly.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Moreover, using a single preference-conditioned actor-critic framework rather than one per objective also reduces the memory costs and training costs associated with maintaining the separate actor-critic networks of \pgx.
elements.sent-2411-044: We show that \pcc outperforms or matches the performance of \pgx across six robotic control \moqd tasks, including newly introduced tri-objective ones (see \Cref{section:tasks ).}
elements.sent-2411-045: The code for \pcc is fully containerised and available at \code.
elements.sent-2411-046: Quality-Diversity algorithms aim to discover collections of solutions that are both high-performing and diverse~\cite{pugh2016quality .}
elements.sent-2411-047: Similar to standard optimisation algorithms, a solution $\theta \in \Theta$ is assessed via a fitness function $f: \Theta \to \mathbb{R}$ that reflects its performance on the task.
elements.sent-2411-048: For example, consider the task of generating an image of a celebrity from a text prompt.
elements.sent-2411-049: In this case, the fitness of a solution could be the CLIP score \cite{clipscore which measures the fidelity of an image to its caption that was used to generate it.}
elements.sent-2411-050: However, an additional central component to \qd algorithms, is the concept of the feature function $\Phi: \Theta \to \mathbb{R ^d$ that characterizes solutions in a meaningful way for the type of diversity desired~\cite{pugh2016quality .}}
elements.sent-2411-051: The feature of a solution $\Phi(\theta_i)$ is a vector that captures some of its notable characteristics, which is then consequently used to quantify its novelty relative to other solutions.
elements.sent-2411-052: In the image generation example, the feature could be the hair length or age of the subject in the photo \cite{cma-mae .}
elements.sent-2411-053: In this example, the \qd algorithm would then aim to generate images in which the subject has a diverse range of hair lengths and ages, and which closely obey the given text prompt used to generate it.
elements.sent-2411-054: One branch of algorithms in the \qd family stems from the \mapelites algorithm~\cite{mapelites , which has gained prominence for its simplicity and effectiveness.}
elements.sent-2411-055: Tessellating the feature space in this manner creates a systematic method for exploring of different niches within this space \cite{cvt .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Each iteration of \mapelites first involves selecting solutions from these niches, creating copies of them and mutating these copies to create new candidate solutions.
elements.sent-2411-057: Then, the fitness and features of the candidate solutions are evaluated, and they are added to the appropriate niches based on their fitness.
elements.sent-2411-058: If the cell corresponding to the new solution's feature vector is unoccupied, the new solution is added to the cell.
elements.sent-2411-059: If the cell is occupied, but the evaluated solution has a higher fitness than the current occupant, it is added to the grid.
elements.sent-2411-061: This process continues for a fixed number of iterations, progressively populating the grid structure with an array of diverse, high-quality solutions.
elements.sent-2411-062: Given a search space $\Theta$ and a feature space $\mathcal{C}$ that has been tessellated into $k$ cells $\mathcal{C _i$, the \mapelites objective, or QD-score~\cite{qdunifying can be formally expressed as: \begin{equation}\max_{\theta\in\Theta} \sum_{i=1}^{k} f(\theta_i), \,\,\text{where} \,\,\forall i, \Phi(\theta_i)\in \mathcal{C}_i\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Multi-Objective (\mo) optimization provides an approach for addressing problems that involve the simultaneous consideration of multiple, often conflicting objectives $F = [f_1, \dots, f_m]$.
elements.sent-2411-064: In \mo problems, objectives often compete with each other, meaning that improving one objective typically comes at the expense of another.
elements.sent-2411-065: For example, in engineering, improving performance might increase cost, and vice versa.
elements.sent-2411-066: To navigate this landscape, the concept of Pareto-dominance is commonly employed to establish a preference ordering among solutions.
elements.sent-2411-067: A solution $\theta_1$ is said to dominate another solution $\theta_2$ if it is equal or superior in at least one objective and not worse in any other \cite{guidetomorl .}
elements.sent-2411-068: That is, $\theta_1 \succ \theta_2$, if $\forall i: f_i(\theta_1) \geq f_i(\theta_2) \wedge \exists j: f_j(\theta_1) > f_j(\theta_2) $.
elements.sent-2411-069: Solutions that are not dominated by any other solutions are termed non-dominated.
elements.sent-2411-070: Given a set of candidate solutions $\mathcal{S}$, the non-dominated solutions of this set $\theta_i \in S$ collectively form a Pareto front, which represents the boundary of achievable trade-offs among objectives.
elements.sent-2411-071: The goal of \mo optimisation is to find an approximation to the optimal Pareto front, which is the Pareto front over the entire search space $\Theta$.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=0.6\linewidth]{Background/figures/pf.png} \caption{Sets of solutions that form approximations to two Pareto fronts. The hypervolume of the outer solutions is larger as they achieve higher performance on the objectives. Likewise, the sparsity metric of the outer solutions will also be higher as they are more evenly spaced.} \label{fig:pfs}
elements.sent-2411-072: There are two metrics, the hypervolume and sparsity metric (see \Cref{fig:pfs ), that play pivotal roles in comprehensively assessing the quality and diversity of solutions within the Pareto front \cite{usinghypervolumes, guidetomorl .}}
elements.sent-2411-073: The hypervolume $\Xi$ of a Pareto front $\mathcal{P}$, measures the volume of the objective space enclosed by a set of solutions relative to a fixed reference point $r$.
elements.sent-2411-074: This metric provides a quantitative measure of the quality and spread of solutions in the objective space and is calculated as \cite{guidetomorl, usinghypervolumes : \begin{equation}\Xi(\mathcal{P}) = \lambda ({\theta \in \Theta \,|\, \exists \, s \in \mathcal{P}, s \succ x \succ r}) \label{eqn:hypervolume}\end{equation} where $\lambda$ denotes the Lebesgue measure.}
elements.sent-2411-075: While the hypervolume metric quantifies the coverage of the objective space by solutions on the Pareto front, sparsity provides complementary information regarding the distribution and evenness of these solutions.
elements.sent-2411-076: It is calculated by evaluating the average nearest neighbour distance among solutions on the Pareto front, given by \cite{guidetomorl : \begin{equation}S(\mathcal{P}) = \frac{1}{|\mathcal{P}| - 1} \sum_{j=1}^m \sum_{i=1}^{|\mathcal{P}| - 1} (\Tilde{P_j}(i) - \Tilde{P_j}(i+1))^2 \label{eqn:sparsity}\end{equation} where $\Tilde{P_j (i)$ denotes the $i$-th solution of the list of solutions on the front $\mathcal{P}$, sorted according to the $j$-th objective and $|\mathcal{P |$ denotes the number of solutions on the front.}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: To ensure that the sparsity is not skewed due to different scales of each of the objectives, the objective functions must be normalised prior to calculating it.
elements.sent-2411-078: A low-sparsity metric indicates that solutions are well-dispersed through the objective space, highlighting the algorithm's ability to provide diverse trade-off solutions.
elements.sent-2411-079: In contrast, a high-sparsity metric suggests that solutions are clustered in specific regions, potentially indicating that the algorithm struggles to explore and represent the full range of possible trade-offs.
elements.sent-2411-080: Recently, policy gradient variations, inspired by single-objective methods, have played a pivotal role in shaping the development of techniques in \moqd.
elements.sent-2411-081: Notably, while \mome (see \Cref{section:moqd ) is a simple and effective \moqd approach, it relies on \ga policy-gradient mutations as an exploration mechanism which makes it inefficient in high-dimensional search spaces.}
elements.sent-2411-082: To overcome this challenge, Multi-Objective MAP-Elites with Policy-Gradient Assistance and Crowding-based Exploration (\pgx) \cite{mome-pgx was recently introduced as an effort to improve the performance and data-efficiency of \mome in tasks that can be framed as a \momdp.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Despite this success, \pgx requires maintaining distinct actor-critic pairs for each objective, which is costly in memory.
elements.sent-2411-084: Moreover, since each actor-critic network pair learns about each of the objective separately, the \pg variations may only provide disjoint gradient information about each of the objectives, and fail to capture nuanced trade-offs.
elements.sent-2411-085: To the best of our knowledge \mome and \pgx are the only existing \moqd algorithms to date.
elements.sent-2411-086: However, we also note of two particularly relevant approaches which have synergies with the \moqd setting.
elements.sent-2411-087: Multi-Criteria Exploration (\mcx) \cite{mcx which uses a tournament ranking strategy to condense a solution's score across multiple objectives into a single value, and then uses a standard MAP-Elites strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-088: Similarly, Many-objective Optimisation via Voting for Elites (\move) \cite{move uses a MAP-Elites grid to find solutions which are high-performing on many-objective problems.}
elements.sent-2411-089: In this method, each cell of the grid represents a different subset of objectives and a solution replaces the existing solution in the cell if it is better on at least half of the objectives for the cell.
elements.sent-2411-090: While both \mcx and \move consider the simultaneous maximisation of many objectives, they both aim to find one solution per cell in the \mapelites grid rather than Pareto fronts for different features.
elements.sent-2411-091: Therefore, we consider their goals to be fundamentally different from the \moqd goal defined in \Cref{eqn:moqd .}
elements.sent-2411-092: At each discrete time step $t$, the agent observes the current state $s_t \in \mathcal{S}$ and takes an action $a_t \in \mathcal{A}$ by following a policy $\pi_\theta$ parameterized by $\theta$.
elements.sent-2411-093: Consequently, the agent transitions to a new state sampled from the dynamics probability distribution $s_{t+1 \sim p(s_{t+1 | s_t, a_t)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-094: The agent also receives a reward vector $\mathbf{r _t = [r_1(s_t, a_t), \dots, r_m(s_t, a_t)]$, where each reward function $r_i: \mathcal{S \times \mathcal{A \to \mathbb{R}$ defines an objective.}}}
elements.sent-2411-095: The multi-objective fitness of a policy $\pi$ is defined as a vector F$(\pi) = [f_1(\pi), ..., f_m(\pi)]$.
elements.sent-2411-096: Here, each $f_i$ represents the expected discounted sum of rewards, calculated as $f_i = \mathbb{E _\pi \left[ \sum_t \gamma^t r_i(s_t, a_t) \right]$ for a given reward function $r_i$.}
elements.sent-2411-097: The discount rate $\gamma \in [0, 1]$ controls the relative weighting of immediate and long-term rewards.
elements.sent-2411-098: In the single-objective case ($m = 1$), the \momdp collapses into a simple Markov Decision Process (\mdp) with scalar rewards, where the goal is to find a policy $\pi$ that maximises the expected discounted sum of rewards or return, $F(\pi) = \mathbb{E _{\pi}\left[ \sum_t \gamma^t r(s_t, a_t) \right]$.}
elements.sent-2411-099: Numerous Reinforcement Learning (\rl) methods have been developed to address the challenge of finding policies that optimize this cumulative reward.
elements.sent-2411-100: One particularly relevant approach is the Twin Delayed Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient algorithm (\tdthree)\cite{td3 .}
elements.sent-2411-101: The \tdthree algorithm belongs to the broader family of actor-critic \rl techniques \cite{actor-critic-survey , which involve two key components: an actor network and a critic network.}
elements.sent-2411-102: The actor network is a policy parameterised by $\phi$, denoted $\pi_\phi$ that is used to interact with the environment.
elements.sent-2411-103: The transitions $(s_t, a_t, r_t, s_{t+1 )$ coming from the interactions with the environment are stored in a replay buffer $\mathcal{B}$ and used to train the actor and the critic.}
elements.sent-2411-104: The critic network is an action-value function parameterised by $\psi$, denoted $Q_\psi$ that evaluates the quality of the actor's actions and helps the agent learn to improve its decisions over time.
elements.sent-2411-105: The critic estimates the expected return obtained when starting from state $s$, taking action $a$ and following policy $\pi$ thereafter, $Q_\psi(s, a) = \mathbb{E _\pi[\sum_t \gamma^t r(s_t, a_t) | s_0 = s, a_0 = a]$.}
elements.sent-2411-106: The \tdthree algorithm, uses a pair of critic networks $Q_{\psi_1 , Q_{\psi_2}$, rather than a single critic network in order to reduce overestimation bias and mitigate bootstrapping errors.}
elements.sent-2411-107: These networks are trained using samples $(s_t, a_t, r_t, s_{t+1 )$ from the replay buffer and then regression to the same target: \begin{equation}y = r(s_t, a_t) + \gamma \min_{i=1,2} Q_{ \psi_i}(s_{t+1}, \pi_{\phi'}(s_{t+1}) + \epsilon) \label{eqn:qtarget}\end{equation} where $Q_{\psi_1' , Q_{\psi_2'}$ and $\pi_{\phi'}$ are target networks that are used in order to increase the stability of the training and $\epsilon$ is sampled Gaussian noise to improve exploration and smoothing of the actor policy.}}
elements.sent-2411-108: The actor network is updated to choose actions which lead to higher estimated value according to the first critic network $Q_{\psi_1}$.
elements.sent-2411-109:
This is achieved via a policy gradient (\pg) update:  egin{equation}\label{eqn:actor_policygradient} 
abla _{\phi} J(\pi_\phi) = \mathbb{E} ig[\,
abla _{\phi}\,\pi _{\phi}(s)\,
abla _a Q_{\psi_1} (s, a)\,|\,_{a=\pi _{\phi}(s)}  ig]\end{equation} These actor \pg updates are executed less frequently than the critic network training in order to enhance training stability.
elements.sent-2411-110: Multi-Objective Evolutionary Algorithms (\moea) evolve a population of potential solutions iteratively over several generations to identify an optimal set of solutions that balance conflicting objectives.
elements.sent-2411-111: At each iteration, solutions are selected from the population and undergo genetic variation (through crossover and mutation operators) and are then added back to the population.
elements.sent-2411-112: Different MOEAs can vary in terms of their specific selection strategies, crossover and mutation operators, population management techniques, and how they maintain diversity in the population \cite{moea_survey .}
elements.sent-2411-113: Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II \nsga \cite{nsga2 and Strength Pareto Evolutionary Algorithm 2 (\spea) \cite{spea2 both use biased selection mechanisms to guide the optimisation process.}}
elements.sent-2411-114: Both methods select solutions that are higher performing and occupy less dense regions of the objective space with higher probability.
elements.sent-2411-115: This guides the population towards higher-performing Pareto fronts, while simultaneously ensuring solutions are well-distributed across the front.
elements.sent-2411-116: Our method, \pcc has synergies with many methods from \moea literature including biased selection and addition mechanisms (see \Cref{section:crowding ) and we refer the interested reading to a comprehensive survey of \moea algorithms for more details \cite{moea_survey .}}
elements.sent-2411-117: However, our method differs from traditional \moea approaches in two significant aspects.
elements.sent-2411-118: First, it employs a \mapelites grid to explicitly maintain solutions that are diverse in feature space while optimising over objectives.
elements.sent-2411-119: Second, it incorporates techniques from reinforcement learning to form gradient-based mutations which help to overcome the limited search power of traditional \ga variations for high-dimensional search spaces \cite{pga .}
elements.sent-2411-120: In multi-objective reinforcement learning (\morl) the expected sum of rewards is a vector $\boldsymbol{J (\pi) = \mathbb{E _{\pi [\sum_t \boldsymbol{r_t ]$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-121: Consequently, there is not a straightforward notion of a reward maximising agent.
elements.sent-2411-122: Single-policy \morl approaches focus on discovering a single policy that achieves a desired trade-off of objectives.
elements.sent-2411-123: Often, this is achieved by employing a scalarization function which transforms the performance on various objectives into a single scalar utility value.
elements.sent-2411-124: For example, many approaches aim to find a policy $\pi$ that maximises the expected weighted sum of rewards, \begin{equation}\label{eqn:weighted_expected_returns} \begin{split} J(\pi, \preference)=\mathbb{E}_{\pi}\Big[\sum_t \preference^\intercal \boldsymbol{r}_t\Big]=\preference^\intercal \mathbb{E}_{\pi}\Big[\sum_t \boldsymbol{r}_t\Big] =\preference^\intercal \boldsymbol{J}(\pi) \end{split}\end{equation} Here, $\preference$ is referred to as a preference, with $\sum_i \omega_i = 1$.
elements.sent-2411-125: The preference quantifies the relative importance of each of the objective functions for the end-user and, when the preference is fixed, we can collapse the \momdp into a single-objective setting that can be optimised with well-established \rl approaches.
elements.sent-2411-126: In single-policy approaches, the challenge arises in determining the preference vector beforehand, as it may prove to be a complex task or may vary among different users \cite{guidetomorl .}
elements.sent-2411-127: Instead, it may be useful to find solutions which are optimal for different preference values so that the user can examine the range of possible solutions that is on offer and then assign their preferences retrospectively \cite{guidetomorl .}
elements.sent-2411-128: With this perspective in mind, multi-policy \morl methods aim to find a set of policies that excel across a range of different preferences \cite{dols, prediction_guided_morl .}
elements.sent-2411-129: Often, each policy in the set is trained using preference-conditioned policy-gradient derived from a multi-objective, preference-conditioned action-value function \cite{dols, prediction_guided_morl, generalised-morl .}
elements.sent-2411-130: Some methods straddle the line between single-policy and multi-policy \morl by seeking a single preference-conditioned policy that can maximise the weighted sum of expected returns (\cref{eqn:weighted_expected_returns ) for any given preference \cite{parisi2016multi, dynamicweights, generalised-morl, scalingparetodecisions .}}
elements.sent-2411-131: This approach offers advantages such as reduced storage costs and rapid adaptability \cite{dynamicweights .}
elements.sent-2411-132: However, while having preference-conditioned policy approaches might be cheaper and more flexible, these methods have been observed to achieve worse performance on the objective functions for any given preference than having specialised policies \cite{prediction_guided_morl .}
elements.sent-2411-133: Our work combines elements of both preference-conditioned and multi-policy approaches.
elements.sent-2411-135: However, within each cell of the \mapelites grid, we adopt a multi-policy approach.
elements.sent-2411-136: While storing many policies in each cell is more costly in terms of memory, relying solely on a single preference-conditioned policy in each grid cell would not offer a straightforward means to assess whether a new solution is superior or not.
elements.sent-2411-137: One possible strategy would be to evaluate each policy on a predefined set of preferences, and replace the policy in the grid if it achieves higher values on those preferences.
elements.sent-2411-138: However, this would require multiple costly evaluations so this approach is not practical.
elements.sent-2411-139: To the best of our knowledge, there is no prior research in multi-objective reinforcement learning (\morl) that actively seeks to diversify behaviours in this manner.
elements.sent-2411-140: While these methods have been observed to be highly-effective black box methods, one key limitation is their lack of scalability to high-dimensional search spaces.
elements.sent-2411-141: In tasks in which solutions are the parameters of a neural network, the search space can be thousands of dimensions and thus traditional \ga variation operators do not provide sufficient exploration power.
elements.sent-2411-142: To address this, many works in single-objective \qd leverage the search power of gradient-based methods in high-dimensional search spaces \cite{pga, pga_empirical_analysis, qdpg, dqd, dqdformdps, dcg .}
elements.sent-2411-143: The pioneer of these methods, Policy-gradient assisted \mapelites (\pga) \cite{pga , combines the \tdthree algorithm with the \mapelites algorithms to apply \qd to high-dimensional robotics control tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-144: In particular, during the evaluation of solutions in \pga, environment transitions are stored and used to train actor and critic networks, using the training procedures explained in \Cref{section:td3 .}
elements.sent-2411-145: Then, \pga follows a normal \mapelites loop except in each iteration, half of the solutions are mutated via \ga variations and the other half are mutated via policy-gradient (\pg) updates.
elements.sent-2411-146: Since \pga, several other \qd algorithms with gradient-based variation operators have been proposed.
elements.sent-2411-147: Some of these are tailored to consider different task settings which have differentiable objective and feature functions \cite{dqd or discrete action spaces \cite{discrete_illumination .}}
elements.sent-2411-148: Other methods use policy gradient updates to improve both the fitness and diversity of solutions \cite{qdpg, dqd, dqdformdps .}
elements.sent-2411-149: A particular method of note is \dcg~\cite{dcg,dcrl which uses policy-gradient variations conditioned on features of solutions.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Similar to \pcc, the motivation for this method is to provide more nuanced gradient information.
elements.sent-2411-151: Conditioning the policy-gradient on the feature value of a solution provides a way to update the solution toward higher performance, given that it has a certain behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-152: However, this method only considered mono-objective problems.
elements.sent-2411-153: Other than \pgx (see \Cref{section:moqd ) we are unaware of gradient-based \qd methods applied multi-objective problems.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.995\linewidth]{Method/figures/mome-p2c.png} \caption{Overview of \pcc algorithm. Pareto Fronts are stored in each cell of a \mapelites grid. At each iteration, a batch of solutions are selected, undergo variation and are added back to the grid based on their performance and crowding-distances. As solutions are evaluated, environment transitions are gathered in a replay buffer and used to train preference-conditioned networks. These networks are used with a preference sampler to perform preference-conditioned \pg updates.} \label{fig:method}
elements.sent-2411-154: In this section, we introduce \pcclong (\pcc), a new \moqd algorithm that learns a single, preference-conditioned actor-critic framework to provide policy-gradient variations in tasks that can be framed as \mdp.
elements.sent-2411-155: The algorithm inherits the core framework of existing \moqd methods, which involves maintaining a Pareto front within each feature cell of a \mapelites grid and follows a \mapelites loop of selection, variation, and addition for a given budget.
elements.sent-2411-156: Building on the approach of \pgx, our method not only employs traditional genetic variation operators but also integrates policy gradient mutations that improve sample-efficiency, particularly in high-dimensional search spaces.
elements.sent-2411-157: Similar to \pgx, \pcc adopts crowding-based selection, which strategically directs exploration towards less explored areas of the search space and also utilizes crowding-based addition mechanisms to promote a continuous distribution of solutions along the Pareto front.
elements.sent-2411-158: Distinct from \pgx, which operates with a separate actor-critic framework for each objective function, \pcc innovates by employing a singular, preference-conditioned actor-critic.
elements.sent-2411-159: This design streamlines preference-conditioned policy gradient variation updates to genotypes, significantly reducing the memory requirements of the algorithm and making it more scalable to problems with a higher number of objectives.
elements.sent-2411-160: Furthermore, \pcc leverages the preference-conditioned actor by injecting it into the main population.
elements.sent-2411-161: A visual representation of the algorithm is depicted in \Cref{fig:method , and the accompanying pseudo-code is provided in \Cref{alg:pseudo .}}
elements.sent-2411-162: Detailed descriptions of each component of \pcc are available in the following sections.
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{\pcc pseudo-code}\label{alg:pseudo} \begin{algorithmic} \State{Input:} \begin{itemize} \item \mome archive $\mathcal{A}$ and total number of iterations $N$ \item PG batch size $b_p$, GA batch size $b_g$ (with $b = b_p+b_g$) and actor injection batch size $b_a$ \end{itemize} \State{\initialisation} \State{Initialise archive $\mathcal{A}$ with random solutions $\theta_k$} \State{Initialise replay buffer $\mathcal{B}$ with transitions from $\theta_k$} \State{Initialise actor and critic networks $\pi_{\phi}$, $\boldsymbol{Q_{\psi_1}}$, $\boldsymbol{Q_{\psi_2}}$} \State{\mainloop} \For{$i = 1 \to N $} \State{\sample} \State{$\theta_1, ..., \theta_{b} \gets \text{crowding\_selection}(\mathcal{A})$} \State{\generate} \State{$\preference_1, ..., \preference_{b_{p}} \gets \text{preference\_sampler}({\theta_{1}, ..., \theta_{b_{p}}})$} \State{$\Tilde{\theta_{1}}, ..., \Tilde{\theta_{b_{p}}} \gets \text{pg\_variation}(\theta_{1}, ..., \theta_{b_{p}}, \preference_1, ... ,\preference_{b_{p}} $)} \State{$\Tilde{\theta_{b_p+1}}, ..., \Tilde{\theta_{b}} \gets \text{ga\_variation}(\theta_{b_p +1}, ..., \theta_{b}$)} \State{$\preference_{b_{p}+1}, ... , \preference_{b_{p} + b_{a}} \gets \text{actor\_sampler}$} \State{$\Tilde{\theta_{b+1}},...,\Tilde{\theta_{b+b_{a}}}\gets \text{actor\_inject}(\pi_\phi, \preference_{b_{p}+1}, ... , \preference_{b_{p}+b_{a}} $)} \State{\offspringeval} \State{$(f_1,...,f_m, d, \text{transitions})\gets \text{evaluate}(\pi_{\Tilde{\theta_{1}}},...,\pi_{\Tilde{\theta_{b+b_{a}}}})$} \State{$\mathcal{B}\gets \text{insert}(\text{transitions})$} \State{$\pi_{\phi}, Q_{\psi_1}, Q_{\psi_2} \gets \text{train\_networks}(\mathcal{B}, \pi_{\phi}, \boldsymbol{Q_{\psi_1}}, \boldsymbol{Q_{\psi_2}})$} \State{\archiveadd} \State{$\mathcal{A}\gets \text{crowding\_addition}(\Tilde{\theta_{b+1}},...,\Tilde{\theta_{b+b_{a}}})$} \State{\updateiter} \State{$i \gets i + 1$} \EndFor \Return{ $\mathcal{A}$} \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-163: In \pcc, following \pgx \cite{mome-pgx , we choose to use biased selection and addition mechanisms.}
elements.sent-2411-164: In particular, when selecting parent solutions from the grid, we first select a cell with uniform probability and then select an individual from the cell's Pareto front with probability proportional to its crowding distance.
elements.sent-2411-165: As defined in \nsga \cite{nsga2 , the crowding distance of a solution is defined as the average Manhattan distance between itself and its k-nearest neighbours, in objective space.}
elements.sent-2411-166: In \pgx, it was shown that biasing solutions in this manner provides an effective method for guiding the optimisation process toward under-explored regions of the solution space.
elements.sent-2411-167: Similarly, we also use a crowding-informed addition mechanisms to replace solutions on the Pareto front.
elements.sent-2411-168: It is important to note that all \moqd methods we consider use a fixed maximum size for the Pareto front of each cell.
elements.sent-2411-169: This is done in order to exploit the parallelism capabilities of recent hardware advances \cite{qdax, qdaxrepo and consequently affords many thousands of evaluations in a short period of time.}
elements.sent-2411-170: However, if a solution is added to a Pareto front that is at already maximum capacity, another solution must also necessarily be removed.
elements.sent-2411-171: In \pcc, following from \pgx, we remove the solution with the minimum crowding distance in order to sparsity of solutions on the front.
elements.sent-2411-172: Further details regarding the crowding-based mechanisms can be found in the \pgx paper \cite{mome-pgx .}
elements.sent-2411-173: To justify these selection and addition mechanisms are still valid for \pcc, we include an ablation of these crowding mechanisms in our ablation study (see \Cref{section:ablation_results ).}
elements.sent-2411-174: However, in \pcc, we do not require a separate actor-critic framework for each objective function.
elements.sent-2411-175: Instead, we use a single actor-critic framework that aims to find a single actor policy to maximise $J(\pi, \preference)=\mathbb{E _{\pi [\sum_t \preference^\intercal\boldsymbol{r _t]$ for any given preference $\preference$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-176: Accordingly, we modify the actor network $\pi_\phi(s)$ to be a conditioned on a preference $\pi_\phi(s|\preference)$.
elements.sent-2411-177: By doing so, the actor network now aims to predict the best action to take from state $s_t$ given that its preference over objectives is $\preference$.
elements.sent-2411-178: In practice, this means that the actor takes its current state $s_t$ concatenated with a preference-vector $\preference$ as input, and outputs an action $a_t$.
elements.sent-2411-179: Training a preference-conditioned actor requires a corresponding preference-conditioned critic that evaluates the performance of the actor based on the actor's preference.
elements.sent-2411-180: In this setting, we take corresponding preference-conditioned action-value function $Q^\pi(s, a | \preference)$ to be: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:moqvalue} \begin{split} Q^\pi(s, a | \preference) = \mathbb{E}_{\pi(\cdot|\preference)}\left[\sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^t \preference^\intercal \boldsymbol{r}(s_t, a_t) \mid s_0 = s, a_0 = a\right] =\preference^\intercal \mathbb{E}_{\pi(\cdot|\preference)}\left[\sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^t \boldsymbol{r}(s_t, a_t) \mid s_0 = s, a_0 = a\right] =\preference^\intercal \boldsymbol{Q}^{\pi}(s, a) \end{split}\end{equation} Here, $\boldsymbol{Q ^\pi(s, a | \preference)$ denotes the preference-conditioned vectorised action-value function.}
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\label{eqn:moqvalue}  Q^\pi(s, a | \preference) = E_{\pi(\cdot|\preference)}\left[\sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^t \preference^\intercal r(s_t, a_t) \mid s_0 = s, a_0 = a\right] =\preference^\intercal E_{\pi(\cdot|\preference)}\left[\sum_{t=0}^{T} \gamma^t r(s_t, a_t) \mid s_0 = s, a_0 = a\right] =\preference^\intercal Q^{\pi}(s, a) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-181: To train this critic network, we modify the target \tdthree algorithm given in \cref{eqn:qtarget to be: \begin{equation}y = \preference^\intercal r(s_t, a_t) + \gamma \min_{i=1,2} \preference^\intercal Q_{ \psi_i}(s_{t+1}, \pi_{\phi'}(s_{t+1}|\preference) + \epsilon|\preference) \label{eqn:mo_target}\end{equation} which we estimate from minibatches of environment transitions $(s_t, a_t, \boldsymbol{r_t , s_{t+1 )$ stored in the replay buffer $\mathcal{B}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-183: In \pcc, for each sampled transition, we uniformly sample a preference and use this to form a single policy gradient update.
elements.sent-2411-184: Since the preference vector assumes that each of the objectives are scaled equally, we normalise the reward values using a running mean and variance throughout the algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-185: Solutions are stored and added to the archive based on unnormalised fitnesses.
elements.sent-2411-187: However, it is not straightforward to determine the best strategy for choosing the value of this vector.
elements.sent-2411-188: In this work, we use the term ``\pg preference sampler" to refer to present the strategy we use for determining the preference that the \pg variation is conditioned on (illustrated in \Cref{fig:method ).}
elements.sent-2411-189: In \pcc, we choose the \pg preference sampler to simply be a random uniform sampler as we found this to be a simple, yet effective strategy.
elements.sent-2411-190: We examine other choices for the \pg preference sampler in our ablation study (\Cref{section:ablation_results ).}
elements.sent-2411-191: In \pga, \pgx and other gradient-based \qd methods, the actor policy has the same shape as the policies stored in the \mapelites grid and so can be regularly injected into the main offspring batch as a genotype, with no additional cost to the main algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-192: However, in \pcc, the policies in the \mapelites grid only take the current state $s_t$ as input, whereas the preference-conditioned actor takes the state concatenated with a preference $[s_t, \preference]$ as input.
elements.sent-2411-193: Therefore, the actor has a different architecture to the policies so cannot be added to the repertoire.
elements.sent-2411-194: In this work, we take a similar approach to the one taken by \dcrl to inject the conditioned actor within the population~\cite{dcrl .}
elements.sent-2411-195: Given the weights $W \in \mathbb{R ^{n \times (|\mathcal{S |+m)}$ and bias $B\in\mathbb{R ^{n}$ of the first layer of the actor network, we note if we fix the value of $\preference$ we can express the value of the $n$ neurons in the first layer as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} W\begin{bmatrix}s_t \preference\end{bmatrix} + B = \begin{bmatrix}W_s W_{\preference}\end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix}s_t \preference\end{bmatrix} + B = W_s s_t + (W_{\preference} \preference + B) \end{aligned}\end{equation} In other words, if the input preference $\preference$ to the actor is fixed, we can reshape the preference-conditioned actor network to be the same shape as the policies in the \mapelites grid by absorbing the weights corresponding to the preference input into the bias term of the first layer.}}}
elements.formula-2411-011: \begin{equation} Ws_t \preference + B = W_s W_{\preference} s_t \preference + B = W_s s_t + (W_{\preference} \preference + B) \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-196: This method provides a cheap approach to use the preference-conditioned actor network to generate offspring which have the same shape as other policies in the grid.
elements.sent-2411-197: To take advantage of this in \pcc, at each iteration we sample $n_a$ preferences from an ``actor preference sampler" (see \Cref{fig:method ) and use them to reshape the actor network into $n_a$ new policies.}
elements.sent-2411-198: In this work, we choose the actor preference sampler to generate $n_a - m$ uniformly sampled preference vectors for exploration, and $m$ one-hot vectors (with a one at the index for each of the objectives) to ensure that fitness in each of the objectives is always pushed.
elements.sent-2411-199: In this section, we describe the evaluation tasks, baselines and metrics we use to evaluate our approach.
elements.sent-2411-200: Importantly, we introduce two new tri-objective \moqd tasks, \antthree and \hopperthree, which allow us to evaluate the capabilities of different \moqd approaches to scale to a larger number of objectives..
elements.sent-2411-201: We also introduce two new \moqd metrics, \moqdsparsityscore and \globalsparsity, which we argue are important ways to assess whether\moqd algorithms are able to achieve smooth sets of trade-offs.
elements.sent-2411-202: These new tasks and metrics form two key contributions of this work.
elements.figure-2411-004:
\caption{Summary of evaluation tasks.} \label{tab:tasks} 0.8{  egin{tabular}{ c | p{2.2cm} | p{2.2cm} | p{2.2cm} | p{2.2cm} | p{2.2cm} | p{2.2cm} } \multicolumn{2}{c|}{\makecell{\includegraphics[width = 0.07	extwidth]{Experiments/figures/ant.png}}} \makecell{\includegraphics[width = 0.07	extwidth]{Experiments/figures/halfcheetah.png}} \multicolumn{2}{c|}{\makecell{\includegraphics[width = 0.07	extwidth]{Experiments/figures/hopper.png}}} \makecell{\includegraphics[width = 0.07	extwidth]{Experiments/figures/walker2d.png}} \addlinespace[0.05cm] \addlinespace Name \makecell{\anttwo} \makecell{\antthree} \makecell{\halfcheetahtwo} \makecell{\hoppertwo} \makecell{\hopperthree} \makecell{\walkertwo} \addlinespace[0.05cm] \addlinespace \makecell{feature} \multicolumn{6}{c}{\makecell{Feet Contact Proportion}} \addlinespace[0.05cm] \addlinespace[0.05cm] Rewards  egin{itemize}[leftmargin=*] \item Forward 
ewline velocity \item Energy consumption \end{itemize}  egin{itemize}[leftmargin=*] \item $x$ velocity \item $y$ velocity \item Energy consumption \end{itemize}  egin{itemize}[leftmargin=*] \item Forward 
ewline velocity \item Energy consumption \end{itemize}  egin{itemize}[leftmargin=*] \item Forward 
ewline velocity \item Energy consumption \end{itemize}  egin{itemize}[leftmargin=*] \item Forward

elements.sent-2411-203: We evaluate our approach on six continuous control robotics tasks, which are summarised in \Cref{tab:tasks .}
elements.sent-2411-204: In these tasks, solutions correspond to the parameters of closed-loop neural network controllers which determine the torque commands given to each of the robot's joints.
elements.sent-2411-205: We use four robot morphologies from the Brax suite \cite{brax .}
elements.sent-2411-206: In all of the tasks, the feature is the proportion of time that each of the robot's legs spends in contact with the ground.
elements.sent-2411-207: Using this characterisation, solutions that have diverse features will exhibit different gaits \cite{nature, mome .}
elements.sent-2411-208: In four of the tasks (\anttwo, \halfcheetahtwo, \hoppertwo, \walkertwo) the aim is to maximise the forward velocity of the robot while minimising its energy consumption \cite{mome, mome-pgx .}
elements.sent-2411-209: However, we also introduce two tri-objective \moqd environments: \antthree and \hopperthree.
elements.sent-2411-210: In the \antthree task, the objectives are the robot's $x$-velocity, $y$-velocity and energy consumption.
elements.sent-2411-211: Hence the goal is to discover controllers that lead to different gaits, and for each of these gaits to find controllers that travel in different directions while minimising the energy cost.
elements.sent-2411-212: In the \hopperthree task, the rewards correspond to the robot's forward velocity, torso height and energy consumption.
elements.sent-2411-213: The corresponding \moqd goal is to therefore find solutions which have different gaits, and for each of these gaits to find controllers that make the hopper jump to different heights or travel forward, while minimising the energy cost.
elements.sent-2411-214: We designed these tasks as they present interesting and realistic objectives, and also provide opportunity to compare \moqd algorithms on tasks with $m>2$.
elements.sent-2411-215: We compare \pcc to five baselines: \pgx, \mome, \pga, \nsga and \spea.
elements.sent-2411-216: In \pga, we convert the multiple objectives into a single one by adding them.
elements.sent-2411-217: To ensure all algorithms have equal population sizes, if we use a grid of $k$ cells with maximum Pareto length of $|\mathcal{P |$ for \moqd methods, we use $k \times |\mathcal{P |$ cells for \pga and a population size of $k \times |\mathcal{P |$ for \nsga and \spea.}}}
elements.sent-2411-218: To report metrics for \pga, \nsga and \spea, we use a passive archive with the same structure as the \moqd methods.
elements.sent-2411-219: At each iteration, we fill the passive archive with solutions found by the algorithm and then calculate metrics on these archives.
elements.sent-2411-220: Importantly, the passive archives do not interact within the primary algorithmic loop, ensuring that there is no effect on the behaviour of the baseline algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-221: We evaluate our method based on six metrics:.
elements.sent-2411-222: The \moqdscore \cite{mome, mome-pgx is the sum of the hypervolumes of the Pareto fronts stored in the archive $\mathcal{A}$: \begin{equation}\sum_{i=1}^{k} \Xi(\mathcal{P}_i), \,\,\text{where} \,\,\forall i, \mathcal{P}_i = \mathcal{P}(x \in \mathcal{A}|\Phi(x)\in C_i)\end{equation} This metric aims to assess if an algorithm can find high-performing Pareto fronts, for a range of features.}
elements.sent-2411-223: We introduce the \moqdsparsityscore, which we define as the average sparsity of each Pareto front $\Xi(\mathcal{P _i)$ of the archive: \begin{equation}\frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} S(\mathcal{P}_i), \,\,\text{where} \,\,\forall i, \mathcal{P}_i = \mathcal{P}(x \in \mathcal{A}|\Phi(x)\in C_i)\end{equation} We introduce this metric in \moqd settings as an attempt to measure whether, for each feature, the algorithm succeeds in finding a smooth trade-off of objective functions.}
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation}\frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} S(P_i), \,\,where \,\,\forall i, P_i = P(x \in A|\Phi(x)\in C_i)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-224: The \globalhypscore is the hypervolume of the Pareto front formed over all of the solutions in the archive (which we term the global Pareto front).
elements.sent-2411-225: The metric assesses the elitist performance of an algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-226: That is, the performance of solutions on the objective functions that are possible when disregarding the solution's feature.
elements.sent-2411-227: By the same reasoning as the \moqdsparsityscore, we also introduce the \globalsparsity, which is the sparsity of the Pareto front formed over all of the solutions in the archive.
elements.sent-2411-228: We calculated the \maxsumscores of objective functions to compare our approach with traditional \qd algorithms which directly aim to maximise this.
elements.sent-2411-229: The \coverage is the proportion of cells in of an archive that are occupied.
elements.sent-2411-230: It reflects how many different features the algorithm is able to uncover (regardless of the performance of the solutions).
elements.sent-2411-231: Since all of the algorithms achieved a similar performance on this metric, we report the results in the supplementary materials.
elements.sent-2411-232: The \moqd metrics (1 and 2) form evaluation methods that most closely align with assessing whether an algorithm achieves the \moqd goal given by \cref{eqn:moqd .}
elements.sent-2411-233: The global metrics (3 and 4) assess the algorithms multi-objective performance, and allow for direct comparison with \mo baselines.
elements.sent-2411-234: Since the sparsity metrics can be impacted by imbalanced scales, we run all of the baselines and find the minimum and maximum of the objectives seen across all of the baselines.
elements.sent-2411-235: We then normalise all of the fitnesses based on these values, and report the sparsity metrics based of the final archives from the normalised fitness values.
elements.figure-2411-005: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Results/figures/results.png} \caption{\moqdscore, \globalhypscore and \maxsumscores (\cref{sec:metrics}) for \pcc compared to all baselines across all tasks. Each experiment is replicated 20 times with random seeds. The solid line is the median and the shaded area represents the first and third quartiles.} \label{fig:main_results}
elements.sent-2411-236: All experiments were run for the same total budget of $4000$ iterations with a batch size of 256 evaluations per generation, corresponding to a total of $1,024,000$ evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-237: We used CVT tessellation \cite{cvt to create archives with $128$ cells, each with a maximum of Pareto Front length of $50$.}
elements.sent-2411-238: For all experiments, we use a Iso+LineDD operator \cite{isoline as the \ga variation operator, with $\sigma_1 = 0.005$ and $\sigma_2 = 0.05$.}
elements.sent-2411-239: The reference points for each environment and the actor-critic training parameters were kept the same across all algorithms and are provided in the supplementary material.
elements.sent-2411-240: In this section, we present the results for all baselines.
elements.sent-2411-241: Each experiment is replicated \replications times with random seeds.
elements.sent-2411-242: We report $p$-values based on the Wilcoxon–Mann–Whitney $U$ test with Holm-Bonferroni correction to ensure statistical validation of the results \cite{wilcoxon1992individual, holm_bonf .}
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Results/figures/sparsity_box_plots.png} \caption{Boxplots to display sparsity metrics calculted on the final archive of \pcc and \pgx over \replications replications. The labels A2, A3, HC2, H2, H3 and W2 correspond to the Ant-2, Ant-3, HalfCheetah-2, Hopper-2, Hopper-3 and Walker-2 environments respectively.} \label{fig:sparsity_box}
elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth]{Results/figures/ablations_results.png} \caption{\moqdscore (\cref{sec:metrics}) for \pcc compared to all ablations across all tasks. Each experiment is replicated \replications times with random seeds. The solid line is the median and the shaded area represents the first and third quartiles.} \label{fig:ablation_results}
elements.sent-2411-243: The experimental results presented in \cref{fig:main_results demonstrate that \pcc outperforms or matches all baselines on all tasks and all metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-244: Crucially, in scenarios where \pcc does not markedly outperform \pgx, it still attains lower sparsity scores (\cref{fig:sparsity_box ), indicating that it achieves smoother array of trade-offs.}
elements.sent-2411-245: Furthermore, \pcc achieves a better \maxsumscores than \pga across all tasks ($p < 10^{-5}$) except Hopper-2 and Walker-2 where it still shows improved but not statistically significant performance.
elements.sent-2411-246: Importantly, we see that \pcc achieves better performance on tri-dimensional tasks, affirming not only its computational efficiency but also its scalability in handling more complex tasks with an increased number of objectives.
elements.sent-2411-247: In our ablation studies, we evaluate \pcc against \pgx together with four distinct modifications to understand the contribution of each component in \pcc.. These ablations include:.
elements.sent-2411-248: Instead of generating 64 policy-gradient offspring and 64 actor-injection offspring at each generation, \noactor produces 128 policy-gradient offspring.
elements.sent-2411-249: The \onehot ablation is the same as \pgx except with a preference-conditioned actor-critic network, rather than separate networks.
elements.sent-2411-250: The results from our ablation studies provide a deeper understanding of the individual components contributing to \pcc's effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-251: Notably, the \noactor ablation, which removes the actor injection mechanism from \pcc, shows an interesting pattern.
elements.sent-2411-252: In the Ant-2 and HalfCheetah-2 ($p < 10^{-4}$), \pcc significantly outperforms the \noactor ablation, suggesting that some tasks which require a more nuanced exploration strategy may benefit greatly from the the actor injection mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-253: Moreover, in all of the other tasks except for Hopper-2, \noactor either matches or falls behind the full \pcc model.
elements.sent-2411-254: Furthermore, the \nocrowding ablation, where \pcc operates without its crowding mechanisms, significantly underperforms compared to the standard \pcc across all tasks ($p < 10^{-4}$).
elements.sent-2411-255: This uniform selection and replacement strategy evidently lack the refined search capabilities provided by crowding, underscoring the importance of these mechanisms in guiding the algorithm towards more diverse and high-quality solutions.
elements.sent-2411-256: Finally, the modifications in preference sampling strategies, as explored in the \keeppref and \onehot ablations, markedly influence \pcc’s performance.
elements.sent-2411-257: The \keeppref ablation, which retains the parent's preference in the policy-gradient variation operator shows no improvement over \pcc across all tasks.
elements.sent-2411-258: The \onehot ablation, employing one-hot preferences in equal batch sizes achieves equal or worse \moqdscore compared to \pcc across all tasks except Hopper-2.
elements.sent-2411-259: These results highlight the critical role of preference management within \pcc and open up a promising avenue for future research.
elements.sent-2411-260: Specifically, developing strategies to predict preferences that might lead to significant hypervolume improvements, could further enhance \pcc's performance, especially in complex multi-objective optimization tasks \cite{prediction_guided_morl .}
elements.sent-2411-261: In this paper, we have introduced a novel algorithm, \pcc, which represents a significant advancement in the field of Multi-Objective Quality-Diversity (MOQD) optimization.
elements.sent-2411-262: Our experiments and ablation studies have demonstrated \pcc's ability to balance multiple objectives effectively, outperforming existing state-of-the-art methods in challenging continuous control environments.
elements.sent-2411-263: One of the key strengths of \pcc is its use of preference-conditioned policy gradient mutations, which not only enhance the exploration process but also ensures an even distribution of solutions across the Pareto front.
elements.sent-2411-264: This approach addresses the limitations of \pgx that struggled with scalability and efficiency.
elements.sent-2411-265: Furthermore, \pcc's ability to perform well in tri-dimensional tasks highlights its scalability and adaptability to more complex and realistic scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-266: Our ablation studies highlight that the strategy for sampling preferences can have a large impact on the performance of \pcc.
elements.sent-2411-267: The exploration of using models to predict which preference will lead to the largest hypervolume gain \cite{prediction_guided_morl presents an exciting direction for further research.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran \appendices.}}
elements.figure-2411-008: \includegraphics[width=0.9\textwidth]{Appendix/coverage_plot.png} \caption{Median coverage performance of \replications seeds, the shaded regions show the inter-quartile range.} \label{fig:coverage}
elements.sent-2411-268: As expected, all Quality-Diversity algorithms achieve a higher coverage score than the MOEA baselines, as they explicitly seek diverse solutions.
elements.sent-2411-269: The same reference points were used for all of the experiments.
elements.figure-2411-009: \caption{Reference points} \begin{tabular}{l | l } Ant-2 [-350, -4500] Ant-3 [-1200, -1200, -4500] HalfCheetah-2 [-2000, -800] Hopper-2 [-50, -2] Hopper-3 [-750, -3, 0] Walker-2 [-210, -15] \end{tabular} \label{tab:refpoints}
elements.sent-2411-270: Table \ref{tab:pghyps presents all of the policy gradient hyperparameters that are used for our algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-271: All hyperparameters were kept the same for each task and for all algorithms which used PG variations.
elements.figure-2411-010: \caption{Policy Gradient Network Hyperparameters} \begin{tabular}{l | l } Replay buffer size 1,000,000 Critic training batch size 256 Critic layer hidden sizes $[256, 256]$ Critic learning rate $3\times 10^{-4}$ Actor learning rate $3\times 10^{-4}$ Policy learning rate $1\times 10^{-3}$ Number of critic training steps 300 Number of policy gradient training steps 100 Policy noise 0.2 Noise clip 0.2 Discount factor 0.99 Soft $\tau$-update proportion 0.005 Policy delay 2 \end{tabular} \label{tab:pghyps}
